coredata.dc:title: Encapsulating carbon dots in polymethyl methacrylate nanoparticles toward improved solid-state fluorescence quantum yield
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Na
Wu, Sha
Shen, Xinyi
He, Pingyuan
He, Ping
Guan, Linbo
Bai, Huai
Xiao, Yao
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we prepare carbon dots (CDs) through hydrothermal method, and then the CDs are encapsulated in polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) nanoparticles through the suspension polymerization of MMA nanodroplets, obtaining a CD/PMMA nanocomposite with a CD concentration of 0.3 wt%. The CDs of the nanocomposite are confined in the PMMA nanoparticles, so there is no excessive resonance energy transfer or direct π-π interaction among the CDs in different nanoparticles, weakening the aggregation caused quenching compared with conventional CD/PMMA composite fabricated by directly blending the CDs with bulk PMMA. Consequently, the quantum yield (QY) of this CD/PMMA nanocomposite is higher than that of the conventional CD/PMMA composite, and even very close to that of the dilute CD solution. This study may provide a universal method to prepare CD/polymer solid-state fluorescent materials with higher QYs compared with conventional CD/polymer composites prepared through direct blending CDs with bulk polymers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Quantum yield
Polymethyl methacrylate
Nanocomposites

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the conventional CD/PMMA composites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: West China Second University Hospital of Sichuan University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Research Seed Fund from West China Second University Hospital of Sichuan University ( K095 ). We would like to thank eceshi ( www.eceshi.com ) for the TEM, DLS, PL spectrum and QY characterizations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) PL emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of pure CDs. The insets of are the high-resolution TEM image of the CD (left) and the DLS curve of the CD/DMF solution (right). The inset of Fig. 1b is the photos of the pure CDs under daylight (upper) and UV light of 365 nm (lower). (c) XPS and (d) FTIR spectrum of the CDs. (e) UV–Vis absorbance, maximum PL excitation and emission spectra and (f) PL emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of the CD/DMF solution (0.0001 wt%). The inset of Fig. 1f is the photos of the CD/DMF solution under daylight (left) and UV light of 365 nm (right).
Schematic illustration of the preparation process of the CD/PMMA nanocomposites. The inset is the photo of the Nanocomposite-2 emulsion.

TEM images of (a) the PMMA nanoparticle emulsion and (b) Nanocomposite-2 emulsion. The insets are the DLS curves of the emulsions. UV–Vis absorbance, maximum PL excitation and emission spectra of (c) the PMMA nanoparticles and (d) Nanocomposite-2. PL emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of (e) the PMMA nanoparticles and (f) Nanocomposite-2. The insets are the photos of them under daylight (upper) and UV light of 365 nm (lower).

(a) Maximum PL excitation and emission spectra and (b) PL emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of Conventional-2. The inset of Fig. 4b are the photos of Conventional-2 under daylight (upper) and UV light of 365 nm (lower).

PL emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of (a) Nanocomposite-1, (b) Nanocomposite-3, (c) Conventional-1 and (d) Conventional-3. The insets are the photos of them under daylight (upper) and UV light of 365 nm (lower).

(a) Normalized PL intensity of the CDs and Nanocomposite-2 after different times of the irradiation of the UV lamp. (b) Cell viability of L929 after incubated with different concentrations of Nanocomposite-2 for 24 h.
Confocal microscope images of the cells stained by Nanocomposite-2.

head.ce:title: Encapsulating carbon dots in polymethyl methacrylate nanoparticles toward improved solid-state fluorescence quantum yield

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, China West Normal University, Nanchong, 637009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, China West Normal University, Nanchong 637009, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guang'an Mental Health Center, Guang'an, 638000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guang'an Mental Health Center, Guang'an 638000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Genetic Disease and Perinatal Medicine and Key Laboratory of Birth Defects and Related Diseases of Women and Children of the Ministry of Education, West China Second University Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610041, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Genetic Disease and Perinatal Medicine and Key Laboratory of Birth Defects and Related Diseases of Women and Children of the Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Genetic Disease and Perinatal Medicine and Key Laboratory of Birth Defects and Related Diseases of Women and Children of the Ministry of Education, West China Second University Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610041, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we prepare carbon dots (CDs) through hydrothermal method, and then the CDs are encapsulated in polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) nanoparticles through the suspension polymerization of MMA nanodroplets, obtaining a CD/PMMA nanocomposite with a CD concentration of 0.3 wt%. The CDs of the nanocomposite are confined in the PMMA nanoparticles, so there is no excessive resonance energy transfer or direct π-π interaction among the CDs in different nanoparticles, weakening the aggregation caused quenching compared with conventional CD/PMMA composite fabricated by directly blending the CDs with bulk PMMA. Consequently, the quantum yield (QY) of this CD/PMMA nanocomposite is higher than that of the conventional CD/PMMA composite, and even very close to that of the dilute CD solution. This study may provide a universal method to prepare CD/polymer solid-state fluorescent materials with higher QYs compared with conventional CD/polymer composites prepared through direct blending CDs with bulk polymers.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a kind of fluorescent spherical or quasi-spherical nanoparticles with carbonaceous structure [1,2]. CDs were firstly reported in 2004 [3], and have been studied and widely employed for solar concentrators [4,5], bio-imaging [6], optoelectronic devices [7], chemical detection [8], information encryption [9] and photocatalysis [10] in recent years, in virtue of their outstanding advantages including tunable photoluminescence (PL), low toxicity, good biocompatibility, low cost and high photo-stability [11–16]. They are a promising alternate for existing fluorescent materials such as rare-earth-based phosphors, organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots [17,18].

CDs have strong PL emission with high fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) in their dilute solutions, but they display very weak PL emission in concentrated solutions and solid state, because excessive resonance energy transfer and/or direct π-π interaction among adjacent layers occur when CDs are close to each other [19–21]. This decrease of PL intensity is called aggregation caused quenching (ACQ), which seriously impedes the application of CDs in many areas like lighting devices [22–24].

Blending CDs with polymers is one of the most effective ways to prepare CD-based solid-state fluorescent materials at present [25]. Because many polymers have unique properties such as high transmittance in UV–visible (UV–Vis) range, ease of engineering and process, good thermal- and photo-stability, excellent mechanical strength, as well as high compatibility with guests [26–30]. Polymer matrices can separate the incorporated CDs to suppress the ACQ, achieving much stronger PL emission compared with pure CDs in solid state. Those polymers for fabricating solid-state fluorescent CD/polymer composites include polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) [31], polyurethane [32], polyvinyl alcohol [33], polyvinyl pyrrolidone [34], polydimethyl siloxane [35], and so on. The PL intensity of CD/polymer composites has two periods as the concentration of CDs in polymer matrices increases. In the first period, the PL intensity increases with the increase of the concentration of CDs, because CDs are far away from each other at this time, the elevated number of CDs gives rise to increased PL intensity. In the second period, the PL intensity decreases with the increase of the concentration of CDs in polymer matrices, since the interaction of CDs is enhanced at this moment, leading to the ACQ, accordingly low QYs [28,36,37]. In a word, the concentration of CDs in polymer matrices displays a critical value, which determines that the QYs of CD/polymer composites are not high, and much lower than that of dilute CD solutions. It is a great challenge to find a method to fabricate novel CD/polymer composites that have higher QYs than conventional CD/polymer composites prepared through direct blending.

In this study, we prepared hydrophobic CDs through hydrothermal method, and the CDs are encapsulated in PMMA nanoparticles after the suspension polymerization of methyl methacrylate (MMA) nanodroplets, obtaining a CD/PMMA nanocomposite with a CD concentration of 0.3 wt%. The CDs of the nanocomposite are confined in the PMMA nanoparticles, which is more easily to weaken the ACQ than the conventional CD/PMMA composites that prepared by directly blending CDs with PMMA. Consequently, the fluorescence QY of this CD/PMMA nanocomposite is higher than that of the conventional CD/PMMA composite with the same CD concentration, even close to that of the CD dilute solution.

Urea was purchased from Chengdu Chron Chemical Co., Ltd., China. MMA was purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd., Japan. Azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN), o-phenylenediamine (PDA), sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS) and sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) were bought from Beijing InnoChem Science & Technology Co., Ltd., China. Butyl acetate and dimethylformamide (DMF) were both bought from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd., China. Methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were both purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., US. Butyl acetate and DMF were chromatographic grade, the others were analytical grade. RPMI Medium 1640 basic medium and Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) were both bought from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., US.

L929 (mouse fibroblasts) was provided by the National Collection of Authenticated Cell Cultures, China. HMrSV5 (human peritoneal mesothelial cell line) was purchased from ORiCells Biotechnolgoy (Shanghai) Co., Ltd., China.

Here, 1.08 g of PDA and 0.18 g of urea were dissolved in 80 g of deionized water, then distributed into eight Teflon-lined autoclaves with a capacity of 25 ml and reacted at 180 °C for 12 h. The product in the autoclaves was filtered and the CDs were obtained by washing the filter residue with warm water for several times and drying at 80 °C for 2 h.

First, PMMA nanoparticle emulsion was prepared through the suspension polymerization of the MMA nanodroplets. In detail, 2.5 g of SDBS and 2.5 g of SDS were dissolved in 50 g of deionized water in a three-necked flask at 50 °C. After that, 5 g of MMA and 0.05 g of AIBN (dissolved in MMA) were added into the flask and reacted at 80 °C for 3 h under stirring and reflux condensation, obtaining the PMMA nanoparticle emulsion. 30 g of the emulsion was precipitated with 15 g of calcium chloride aqueous solution (10 wt%) and 30 g of ethanol, then filtrated to get the precipitate. The PMMA nanoparticles were acquired by drying the precipitate at 70 °C for 8 h.

The CDs were dispersed in 5 g of MMA before the suspension polymerization. The CD/PMMA nanocomposites were prepared through the same process as the PMMA nanoparticles. The CD/PMMA nanocomposite emulsions were centrifugaed and then filtrated to remove the unwrapped CDs. After that, 30 g of the emulsions was precipitated with 15 g of calcium chloride aqueous solution (10 wt%) and 30 g of ethanol, then filtrated to get the precipitate. The CD/PMMA nanocomposites were acquired by drying the precipitate at 70 °C for 12 h. The nanocomposites prepared from 0.01, 0.015 and 0.02 g of the CDs were named as Nanocomposite-1, Nanocomposite-2 and Nanocomposite-3. Their CD concentrations are 0.2, 0.3 and 0.4 wt%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the conventional CD/PMMA composites
ce:section.ce:para: The conventional CD/PMMA composites were prepared through solution polymerization. The CDs and 0.05 g of AIBN were dissolved in 5 g of MMA and 5 g of butyl acetate in a three-necked flask, and reacted at 80 °C for 3 h under stirring and reflux condensation, obtaining the CD/PMMA solutions. The solutions were dried at 100 °C for 48 h and then at 100 °C under vacuum for 10 h to acquire the conventional CD/PMMA composites. The conventional composites prepared from 0.01, 0.015 and 0.02 g of CDs were named as Conventional-1, Conventional-2 and Conventional-3. Their CD concentrations are 0.2, 0.3 and 0.4 wt%, respectively.

The MTT assay was performed based on our previous study [4]. L929 cells were cultured on a 96-well plate in an incubator at 37 °C overnight. Each well had 0.2 ml of 1640 basic medium. Subsequently, 0.2 ml of Nanocomposite-2/1640 basic medium dispersion with different concentrations (0.125, 0.25 and 0.5 mg ml−1) was added into each well to replace the original medium, and incubated for 24 h. After that, 0.01 ml of MTT (5 mg ml−1) was added into each well and incubated for 4 h. Then the culture supernatant was removed and 0.2 ml of DMSO was added into each well to dissolve formazan crystals. The cell viability was evaluated by referring to the control group.

The preparation process for the live cell imaging was also based on our previous study [4]. The mediums for L929 and HMrSV5 were 1640 and DMEM, respectively. The cells were seeded on confocal microscope culture dishes containing 1 ml of the mediums and kept in an incubator at 37 °C overnight. Subsequently, 1 ml of the mediums and 25 μl of the Nanocomposite-2/DMSO solution (10 mg ml−1) were added into each dish to replace the original mediums, and incubated for 24 h. The Nanocomposite-2/DMSO solution should be kept under ultrasonic for 30 min in advance. After that, the cells were washed by the mediums twice, then 1 ml of the fresh mediums was added into each dish.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of the samples were shot with a JEM-2100 F high-resolution field emission TEM (JEOL, Japan). The dynamic light scattering (DLS) of the CDs was detected with a Zetasizer Nano S90 nanoparticle size potentiometer (Malvern Instruments, UK). The DLS of the emulsions was observed with a BI-200SM DLS particle size analyzer (Brookhaven Instruments Corporation, US). The absolute fluorescence QYs and steady-state fluorescent spectra of the samples were acquired form a FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instrument, UK). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the sample was detected with an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, US). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of the sample was recorded by a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, US). The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the samples were obtained using a UV-2600 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU, Japan). The confocal microscope images of the cell samples were obtained on an Olympus FV1000 laser scanning confocal microscope (Olympus, Japan).Fig. 1 a.

The CDs in the current study were fabricated based on the preparation process of our previous study with modification [24]. The difference between the CDs in the current and previous study is the particle size. The CDs are hydrophobic and DMF is one of their best solvents. As exhibited in Fig. 1a, the TEM image indicates the CDs are spherical/quasi-spherical nanoparticles that isolated from each other. The calculation result based on the TEM image indicates the particle size of the CDs is between 0.26 and 5.40 nm, and the average size is 1.72 nm. As shown in the inset (left) of Fig. 1a, the high-resolution TEM image declares the CD has clear lattice fringe with a spacing of 0.21 nm. The DLS curve of the CD/DMF solution (0.0001 wt%) in the inset (right) of Fig. 1a manifests the particle size of the CDs is in the range between 0.62 and 1.29 nm, and the average particle size is 0.96 nm. It can be seen in Fig. 1b the pure CDs have no PL emission peaks in the excitation wavelength range from 360 nm to 480 nm, which means the CDs have serious ACQ effect. The inset of Fig. 1b evidences the pure CDs are dark brown powder under daylight, and present no PL emission under the irradiation of UV light of 365 nm.

The XPS of the pure CDs was shown in Fig. 1c. As can be seen in Fig. 1c, the CDs have three XPS peaks located at 532, 399 and 284 eV, corresponding to O1s, N1s and C1s, respectively. The FTIR spectrum of the pure CDs was demonstrated in Fig. 1d. The FTIR absorption peaks at 3435 and 1633 cm−1 are assigned to the N–H stretching vibration and bending vibration of the amino groups, respectively. While the peaks at 1497 and 758 cm−1 belong to the skeleton vibration and C–H bending vibration of the benzene rings, respectively.

The UV–Vis absorbance, maximum PL excitation and emission spectra of the CD/DMF solution are disclosed in Fig. 1e. The CD/DMF dilute solution has three UV–Vis absorbance peaks that located at 267, 363 and 420 nm. The maximum excitation and emission peaks of the CD solution are located at 429 and 518 nm, respectively. As unveiled in Fig. 1f, the emission peaks of the CD dilute solution are all located near 518 nm when the excitation wavelength varies in the range from 360 to 480 nm, demonstrating excitation-independent emission, which means the CDs have single emission center. The QY of the CD/DMF solution is 10.99%, evincing the ACQ is avoided in the dilute solution. As shown in the inset of Fig. 1f, the CD dilute solution is transparent under daylight, and exhibits light green fluorescence under the UV light of 365 nm.

The preparation process of the CD/PMMA nanocomposites is schematically illustrated in Fig. 2 . MMA containing the hydrophobic CDs and AIBN forms nanodroplets in the emulsion with the help of the emulsifiers (SDBS and SDS). After the suspension polymerization initiated by AIBN, the MMA nanodroplets turn into PMMA nanoparticles, and the CDs are encapsulated in the nanoparticles resulting in the CD/PMMA nanocomposites. It can be seen from the inset of Fig. 2 the CD/PMMA nanocomposite emulsion is a light brown dispersion.

The TEM image of Fig. 3 a implies the PMMA nanoparticles are spherical particulates and dispersed in the emulsion. The DLS curve of the inset of Fig. 3a suggests the particle size of the PMMA nanoparticles is in the range between 28.7 and 76.3 nm, and the average particle size is 43.9 nm. The data in Fig. 3b declare Nanocomposite-2 is also spherical nanoparticles dispersed in the emulsion, the particle size range of which is from 32.3 to 79.3 nm, and the average particle size is 50.6 nm. These data indicate the addition of the CDs slightly increases the particle size of the PMMA nanoparticles.

As shown in Fig. 3c, the PMMA nanoparticles have two UV–Vis absorbance peaks located at 218 and 262 nm, which may attributed to the n-π* transition of carbonyl groups [38]. The maximum excitation and emission peaks of the PMMA nanoparticles are located at 359 and 423 nm, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 3d, Nanocomposite-2 has the same UV–Vis absorbance curve as the PMMA nanoparticles, this may be because the UV–Vis absorbance of the CDs in Nanocomposite-2 (only 0.3 wt%) is undetectable. While the maximum excitation and emission peaks of Nanocomposite-2 are not the same as those of the PMMA nanoparticles, which are located at 397 and 548 nm, respectively. It can be seen in Fig. 3e the PMMA nanoparticles display excitation-dependent emission. As the excitation wavelength increases in the range from 360 to 460 nm, the maximum emission wavelength of the PMMA nanoparticles gradually shifts red. As exhibited in the inset of Fig. 3e, the PMMA nanoparticles have a white appearance under daylight, and show negligible fluorescence under the UV light of 365 nm. As demonstrated in Fig. 3f, Nanocomposite-2 has the same excitation-independent emission as the CD dilute solution, the emission peaks of Nanocomposite-2 are all located near 548 nm when the excitation wavelength increases in the range from 360 to 460 nm. It can be seen in the inset of Fig. 3f Nanocomposite-2 is beige under daylight, and shows yellowish green fluorescence under the UV light of 365 nm. Notably, the QY of Nanocomposite-2 is 10.21%, which is very close to that of the CD dilute solution.

For comparison, the PL properties of Conventional-2 (0.3 wt%) are studied. As revealed in Fig. 4 a, the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of Conventional-2 are located at 468 and 539 nm, respectively. As presented in Fig. 4b, Conventional-2 shows excitation-independent emission in the excitation wavelength range from 360 to 460 nm. The inset of Fig. 4b suggests Conventional-2 is dark brown under daylight and exhibits weak green fluorescence under the UV light of 365 nm. The QY of Conventional-2 is 2.46%. The dada in Fig. 4 testify Conventional-2 has much lower QY than nanocomposite-2 with the same concentration of the CDs. As discussed above, the CDs of the nanocomposite were encapsulated in the PMMA nanoparticles during the suspension polymerization of the MMA nanodroplets, there was no excessive resonance energy transfer or direct π-π interaction among the CDs in the different nanoparticles. While the CDs in the conventional composite were encapsulated in the bulk PMMA after the solution polymerization of the MMA solution, so the excessive resonance energy transfer and direct π-π interaction among the CDs were more than those in the nanocomposite, leading to stronger ACQ, consequently, lower QY.

In order to verify whether Nanocomposite-2 possesses the optimized QY, the PL properties of Nanocomposite-1 (0.2 wt%), Nanocomposite-3 (0.4 wt%), Conventional-1 (0.2 wt%) and Conventional-3 (0.4 wt%) are investigated in the current study. As shown in Fig. 5 a, Nanocomposite-1 shows excitation-independent emission and the emission peaks are all located near 536 nm when the excitation wavelength is in the range between 360 and 460 nm. The QY of Nanocomposite-1 is 2.23%. The inset of Fig. 5a indicates Nanocomposite-1 is beige under daylight and presents light green fluorescence under the UV light of 365 nm. As unveiled in Fig. 5b, the emission peaks of Nanocomposite-3 are all located near 545 nm as the excitation wavelength varies in the range from 360 to 460 nm. The QY of Nanocomposite-3 is 3.36%. The inset of Fig. 5b implies Nanocomposite-3 is beige under daylight and shows yellowish green fluorescence under the UV light. It can be seen from Fig. 5c Conventional-1 owns excitation-independent emission, and the emission peaks are all located near 542 nm when the excitation wavelength is in the range between 360 and 460 nm. The QY of Conventional-1 is just 1.89%. The inset of Fig. 5c manifests Conventional-1 is brown under daylight and has weak yellowish green fluorescence under the UV light. As demonstrated in Fig. 5d, the emission peaks of Conventional-3 are all located near 532 nm, and the QY of Conventional-3 is just 1.67%. The inset of Fig. 5d declares Conventional-3 is dark brown under daylight and emits weak green fluorescence under the UV light. The data in Fig. 5 confirm Nanocomposite-2 should have the optimized QY compared to the other CD/PMMA nanocomposites and conventional composites.

The photo-stability of the CDs and Nanocomposite-2 was investigated using a UV lamp of 365 nm/30 W in this study. The decrease of the PL intensity of the CDs and Nanocomposite-2 after different times of continuous irradiation of the UV lamp is demonstrated in Fig. 6 a. As can be seen in Fig. 6a, the PL intensity of the CDs and Nacomposite-2 does not decrease after 30 min of the irradiation of the UV lamp. After 60 min of the irradiation, the PL intensity of the CDs and Nanocomposite-2 decrease by 1.5% and 0.7%, respectively. And after 90 min of the irradiation, the PL intensity of them decrease by 4.1% and 1.3%, respectively. These data indicate the CDs and Nanocomposite-2 both have good photo-stability. Furthermore, the PMMA nanoparticles even enhance the photo-stability of the CDs.

In the current study, Nanocomposite-2 was employed for live-cell imaging. The MMT experimental data is exhibited in Fig. 6b. As shown in Fig. 6b, when the concentrations of Nanocomposite-2 in the media are 0.125, 0.25 and 0.5 mg ml−1, the cell viability after 24 h of incubation is 121.3%, 112.8% and 93.8%, respectively. These data indicate Nanocomposite-2 has extremely low cytotoxicity. The confocal microscope images of the cells incubated with Nanocomposite-2 are exhibited in Fig. 7 . The concentration of Nanocomposite-2 in the mediums for cell imaging is 0.25 mg ml−1. As shown in Fig. 7, L929 and HMrSV5 cells stained with Nanocomposite-2 both emit green fluorescence under the excitation of 488 nm, and display red fluorescence under the excitation of 594 nm. The images of Fig. 7 prove Nanocomposite-2 has good ability for live-cell imaging.

In this paper, the CD/PMMA nanocomposites with different CD concentrations that demonstrate solid-state fluorescence are fabricated successfully. These nanocomposites are prepared by encapsulating the CDs in the PMMA nanoparticles through the suspension polymerization of the MMA nanodroplets. The average particle size of the CD/PMMA nanocomposite with a CD concentration of 0.3 wt% (Nanocomposite-2) is 50.6 nm. The fluorescence QY of Nanocomposite-2 is 10.21%, which is higher than that of the other CD/PMMA nanocomposites and conventional CD/PMMA composites, even very close to that of the dilute CD/DMF solution with a CD concentration of 0.0001 wt%. The CDs of the nanocomposites are confined in the PMMA nanoparticles, which suppresses the ACQ compared with the conventional composites, resulting in improved solid-state fluorescence QY. Additionally, Nanocomposite-2 has very low cytotoxicity, and shows remarkable ability for live-cell imaging.

Na Li: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Sha Wu: Data curation, Investigation. Xinyi Shen: Software, Data curation. Pingyuan He: Investigation. Ping He: Supervision, Investigation. Linbo Guan: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Huai Bai: Supervision, Project administration. Yao Xiao: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Research Seed Fund from West China Second University Hospital of Sichuan University (K095). We would like to thank eceshi (www.eceshi.com) for the TEM, DLS, PL spectrum and QY characterizations.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots based photocatalysis for environmental applications
